result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a tin roof in a house I’ve never actually lived in but somehow remember perfectly. It’s 3:17 a.m. and the clock is lying. Time feels stretched, like warm taffy pulled between two indifferent hands. I’m sitting at a wooden table older than my grandfather, drinking coffee that tastes like forgiveness and burnt regret. The mug says “World’s Okayest Dad” even though I don’t have children. Maybe the mug knows something I don’t.

Outside, the rain argues with the wind in a language that predates nouns. Every drop is a small rebellion against gravity. I respect that. I’ve spent most of my life trying to fall upward.

There’s a typewriter on the table that types by itself sometimes. Right now it’s composing a letter to my future self that begins: “Dear idiot, stop apologizing to people who already forgave you.” The carriage return sounds like a judge slamming a gavel made of nostalgia. I don’t read the rest. Some messages are meant to arrive when you’re ready to be insulted by your own wisdom.

A cat that isn’t mine jumps onto my lap. Her name is Tuesday even though it’s clearly a Thursday. She smells like ozone and secrets. When she purrs it sounds like an old vinyl record of the universe clearing its throat. I scratch behind her ears and she grants me temporary permission to exist loudly.

I think about all the versions of me that didn’t make it to this kitchen. The one who joined the circus. The one who said yes instead of sorry. The one who learned how to cry in front of other men without turning it into a joke. They’re all standing in the doorway wearing my face at different degrees of disappointment and pride. We nod at each other like veterans who don’t need to talk about the war anymore.

The refrigerator hums a minor chord. Inside there’s only half a jar of pickles, a single slice of American cheese sweating in its plastic, and a mysterious Tupperware containing what might be either three-day-old pasta or a small civilization that’s learned to govern itself. I salute it quietly. Every forgotten leftover is a Lazarus waiting for its miracle.

My hands don’t look like mine tonight. They’re bigger, scarred in places I don’t remember earning. There’s ink under three fingernails and a callus from holding on too tightly to things that wanted to leave. These hands have written love letters and grocery lists and one truly unhinged email at 2 a.m. that I thankfully never sent. They’ve also flipped off God, petted dying dogs, and once tried to catch a falling star only to come away with a handful of atmosphere and embarrassment.

The rain stops like someone turned off a faucet in heaven. The sudden silence is so complete I can hear my own blood having opinions. It wants to travel to new countries. It wants to fall in love with an artery it hasn’t met yet. It’s tired of this neighborhood.

I stand up. The chair makes a sound like it’s relieved to be rid of me. Tuesday judges me with ancient Egyptian eyes then forgives me because cats understand that humans are mostly just chaotic weather patterns with anxiety.

Walking to the window, I see my reflection wearing an expression I’ve never practiced in mirrors. It looks like hope wearing work boots. Behind my reflected face, the sky is doing that thing where it decides whether to blush pink or stay melancholic gray. The trees are gossiping about yesterday’s wind. A bird flies by carrying what looks like a tiny piece of tomorrow in its beak.

I press my forehead against the cold glass and fog it with a truth I’ve been avoiding: I am simultaneously the biggest disappointment and the proudest achievement of my own life. Both things fit in the same chest. They sleep in the same bed. They fight over the remote and share the same bad jokes.

The typewriter dings again. This time it’s typed only one sentence:

“You were never supposed to be normal. Stop haunting yourself with that particular ghost.”

I laugh until I cough. The laugh tastes like the coffee and the rain and the possibility that maybe, just maybe, the world isn’t a problem to be solved but a ridiculously long inside joke that we’re all still learning the timing for.

Somewhere, a train whistle blows even though there are no trains for fifty miles. Some things exist purely to remind us that distance is negotiable and longing is just love wearing a disguise.

I leave the coffee mug on the table like an offering. The cat follows me to the door wearing the expression of someone who’s seen this movie before and knows the ending is negotiable.

Outside, the air smells like wet cedar and fresh starts. My feet find a rhythm older than music. Left, right, wonder, fear, left, right, courage, repeat.

I don’t know where I’m going.

But the road knows my name.

And for once, that feels like enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
